7 min read
Just now

--

Why AI-driven automation isn’t a threat to your craft — it’s the exact infrastructure needed to shift your value upstream.

If Artificial Intelligence handles the bulk of our tactical execution, what is actually left for us? And how does collaboration among disciplines change?
For the past decade, I’ve immersed myself in the world of human-centred design and user research across various product companies including Glovo, OLX, and most recently, Booksy. A recurring question has echoed in meeting rooms, Slack channels, and informal reflections with colleagues in recent years:
“If AI takes over the tactical heavy lifting of the researcher, the designer, the product manager etc. what happens to us as professionals?”
Sounds familiar? Technology is moving at a tremendously fast pace, and what holds today might shift tomorrow. However, looking at how corporate operational models are adapting right now, I see this transformation not as a threat but as a new window of opportunity. It’s a chance to build what I call ‘Augmented Synergies’.
Was the Pre-AI Model Already Cracking?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPTTo get a pulse on where we are headed, it’s important to assess where we came from honestly. Rewind just a couple of years. For example, the product discovery phase, in my experience, often relied on a strictly hyper-compartmentalised three-lane model:

• 🔎 Research generated insights, largely on demand;

• ✏️ Design translated ideas into prototypes and interfaces, focusing on execution;

• 🏁 Product Management held the ultimate decision-making power over the roadmap.
This rigid separation naturally ended up normalising a silo mentality. This isn’t a story about one company — it’s a pattern I recognised widely. Designers and researchers were frequently brought to the table far too late — after major strategic decisions had already been finalised. Meanwhile, Product Managers found themselves pulled apart by the competing demands of discovery and delivery, often unintentionally reverting to glorified project managers tracking timelines rather than steering a visionary North Star.
Underpinning this entire framework was one widely shared principle: engineering time was the most precious asset to protect. Every other discipline revolved around ensuring developers time wasn’t slowed down or wasted.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPTBut AI has fundamentally broken that condition. With tools like Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot (you name it), coding has become universally accessible. While quality is still debatable, the entry bar has lowered— not because engineering expertise matters less, but because the system no longer holds the same way. And when the fundamental premise of a system changes, the entire operational model must be rewritten.
Lower Bars, Higher Value
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPTToday, the tactical execution that used to define our unique value is, to a significant degree, democratisable to other roles:

• Anyone can prompt an LLM to write a “good enough” user survey or interview script;

• Anyone can use platforms like Lovable or Claude Design to spin up a decent UI using an internal design system;

• Anyone can rely on LLMs to compile a functional roadmap or a set of OKRs based on existing data;

• Anyone can write working code with AI Assistance (vibe coding).
So, if the tactical outputs become available a couple of clicks and prompts away, where does the value of experts go? It shifts upstream.
Let’s zoom in on User Research as an example, as this is what I’ve been focusing on for the past several years.
User Research: Systemic Multiplier, thanks to AI
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPTIn this new paradigm, User Research also undergoes a significant evolution. Success is no longer measured by the sheer volume of research projects completed, but by the ability to act as strategic partners & enabling system orchestrators.
We are shifting from a team that manually uncovers insights to a function that builds the organization’s scalable capacity to learn from its users, timely.
Let’s look at examples from the past year capturing this continuous shift:
1. AI-Powered Continuous Discovery & Insight Hubs
To make learning from customers widely accessible, the Research Team built an infrastructure that allows product trios (PMs, Designers, and Engineers) to conduct lightweight user research autonomously, with regular cadence.
To make this sustainable and operationally smoother, we utilised Google’s NotebookLM. By feeding transcripts, audio, and video files into organised notebooks, we built a decentralised “Insight Hub”. Product teams could query years of accumulated qualitative data in seconds, instantly killing the dreaded Slack message: “Hey, do we know anything about topic X?”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Dummy-data example of the Insights Hub in NotebookLM for the Continuous Discovery (User Days) in Motors Vertical at OLX. Each Vertical had one dedicated NotebookLM centralising all research conducted over time, with periodical audits by the Research Team to ensure quality.2. Blind Spotter
We organised an internal hackathon using Lovable to actively think about how to elevate our research team’s practice in an AI-powered world. One of the prototypes built was called Blind Spotter: By uploading interview transcripts, the tool automatically maps out a complete customer journey — complete with emotional curves and friction points — while identifying cognitive biases or research blind spots that need further exploration.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Dummy-data example of the Blind Spotter prototype concept created by in Lovable at OLX3. The AI Trainer
To replace time-consuming, unscalable research consultations with Product Teams as “Office Hours” calendar blocks, we also prototyped an AI-driven UX Research Trainer by following publicly available best practices like Nielsen Norman Group’s ones. This provides product teams with 24/7 tactical guidance on demand and frees up researchers’ capacity to double down on strategic bets.
Democratisation or self-service mechanics don’t have to be a threat to our crafts; it is the very infrastructure that can free us up to laser-focus on what matters.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Early prototype of the UX Research Trainer concept by in Lovable at OLXThe Frontier: Intentionality and Synthetic Users
We cannot touch upon AI in research without addressing the elephant in the room: Synthetic Users. While the concept of AI-generated users may trigger initial scepticism, there is undeniable potential if approached with rationale.
The global synthetic data generation market is projected to reach $4.6B by 2032, according to 360iResearch, laying the foundational infrastructure for the rapid expansion of AI-driven synthetic user testing and research. Furthermore, Gartner predicts that 75% of businesses will use GenAI for synthetic customer data by 2026, a massive leap from under 5% in 2023. Instead of getting lost in the debate about whether we should or shouldn’t use them, I find it most important to understand them deeply enough to try to unleash all the benefits the concept promises.
The more I dive into the field myself, the more I find that framing the approach as synthetic personas anchored in a corpus of rich, qualitative, ethnographic data curated internally, fed by regular research as a constant stream, might be the real deal.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPTSynthetic personas are not meant to replace human contact or stop real research. Whatever short-term gains in time-to-insight they offer, they cannot sustain customer-centric organizations alone aiming to thrive and deliver long-term value.
It’s about opening a new path for low-risk, early-stage exploratory concept testing or surveys, where synthetic personas may drastically accelerate velocity without sacrificing data integrity entirely.
The ultimate guardrail here is intentionality. As development cycles shrink across engineering and design, insights must keep pace so research doesn’t become a corporate bottleneck. Synthetic Personas represent a high-potential space to unlock that. This is exactly the space I’m now exploring at Booksy — still early days, but I’m excited to test whether the approach can hold up to that bar of intentionality in this new context! 🧠
What Remains for Us?
As the Nielsen Norman Group noted in their State of UX report 2026, research is becoming more important, not less, precisely because of AI.
As “AI fatigue” spreads and products become oversaturated with automated features, users are actively demanding authenticity and human connection. Our role as digital leaders is to define what that authenticity feels like, mapping out conversational nuances, agentic interfaces, and deeply non-deterministic personalised experiences.
I don’t have this fully figured out yet, and I think that’s the point. Agentic interfaces break the old script: there’s no button to click anymore, just a message, a conversation. So what does the user expect to happen next? What tone of voice earns trust instead of triggering “AI fatigue”? Should the interface feel like an avatar, a mirror of the user themselves, or something else entirely? I think Research’s job right now is to sit inside that discomfort and ask better questions before the rest of the org settles for the easy ones.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPTWe are living through a historical transition where a definitive playbook for collaboration across crafts doesn’t exist yet. Take ownership, look at the system holistically, and design the new playbook yourself within your organisation, cross-functionally.
What remains is the strategic work and judgement that truly counts: Let’s make it matter.
